title: Wörter im Satz 2 (Word order in sentences 2)

sections.title: 1. The Golden Rule of German Word Order
sections.description: In a standard statement, the conjugated verb always occupies Position 2. Any other part of the verb (infinitives, prefixes, or participles) is pushed to the End Position.

sections.title: 2. Grammar Categories & Examples
sections.description: Different grammar structures place parts of the verb at the end of the sentence.

categories.description: Modal verbs (wollen, müssen, können, etc.) express ability, necessity, or desire. The modal verb is conjugated in Position 2, and the main action verb goes to the end in its basic form (infinitive).
categories.rule: Rule: Conjugated Modal Verb (Pos 2) ... Main Verb (End).

examples.translation: They want to take a trip to Germany.
examples.explanation: Why? "Wollen" is the helping verb that carries the tense/person, while "machen" (to do/make) completes the meaning at the end.

categories.description: Some German verbs have prefixes (like um-, auf-, ein-, aus-). When conjugated, the prefix "breaks off" and flies to the end of the sentence.

examples.explanation: Why? The verb is umziehen. The "um" is a separable prefix, so it must land at the very end.

categories.description: When two verbs are used together (like "go swimming"), the movement verb is conjugated in Position 2, and the activity verb stays at the end.

examples.translation: We are going swimming this afternoon.
examples.explanation: Why? "Gehen" indicates the movement/intent, while "schwimmen" defines the action.

categories.description: Certain nouns and verbs function together as a single unit of meaning (e.g., Tennis spielen, Deutsch lernen).

examples.german: Heute Abend spiele ich mit meiner Freundin Tennis.
examples.translation: Tonight I am playing tennis with my girlfriend.
examples.explanation: Why? Even though "Tennis" is a noun, it is treated as part of the verb action "to play tennis," so it takes the end position.

categories.description: In the past tense (Perfekt), you use a helping verb (haben or sein) in Position 2 and the Past Participle (Partizip II) at the end.

examples.explanation: Why? The "ge-" form of the verb (eingekauft) always anchors the end of a Perfect tense sentence.

sections.title: 3. Sentence Variation (Flexible Position 1)
sections.description: German is flexible about what starts a sentence, as long as the verb stays in Position 2. You can emphasize Time or Object by putting them in Position 1.

content.example_intro: Example: "I gave my brother a CD yesterday."

examples.german: Ich habe meinem Bruder gestern eine CD geschenkt.
Gestern habe ich meinem Bruder eine CD geschenkt.
Meinem Bruder habe ich gestern eine CD geschenkt.

content.note: Note: Regardless of what starts the sentence, the conjugated verb (habe) is always the 2nd element, and the participle (geschenkt) is always last.
